questions.text: A company that owns or controls production in more than one nation is called a:
questions.explanation: MNCs set up offices and factories for production in regions where they can get cheap labor and other resources.

questions.text: MNCs set up production units close to markets because it helps in:
questions.explanation: Proximity to markets reduces logistics costs and allows for quicker response to consumer needs.

questions.text: The money that is spent to buy assets such as land, building, machines and other equipment is called:
questions.explanation: Investment is made with the hope that these assets will earn profits.

questions.text: Investment made by MNCs is called:
questions.explanation: Any investment that comes from a company based in another country is termed foreign investment.

questions.text: What is the most common route for MNC investments in developing countries?
questions.explanation: Buying local companies is the fastest way for MNCs to expand production and gain market share.

questions.text: Cargill Foods, a very large American MNC, bought over which Indian company?
questions.explanation: Parakh Foods had built a large marketing network in India, which Cargill then utilized.

questions.text: Foreign trade creates an opportunity for the producers to reach beyond the ______ markets.
questions.explanation: Producers can sell their goods not only in markets located within the country but can also compete in markets elsewhere.

questions.text: With foreign trade, for the buyers, import of goods produced in another country is one way of:
questions.explanation: Foreign trade leads to integration of markets across countries.

questions.text: Globalisation is the process of rapid ______ between countries.
questions.explanation: Globalisation involves increased movement of goods, services, investments, and people.

questions.text: What has been one major factor that has stimulated the globalisation process?
questions.explanation: Developments in transportation and information technology (IT) have made global connection easier.

questions.text: The use of 'containers' for transport of goods has led to:
options: Reduced port handling costs and increased speed
questions.explanation: Containers allow goods to be loaded onto ships, railways, and trucks without repeated handling.

questions.text: IT (Information Technology) has played a major role in spreading ______ across countries.
questions.explanation: Services like call centers and software development can be managed globally using the internet.

questions.text: Removing barriers or restrictions set by the government is what is known as:
questions.explanation: Liberalisation allows businesses to make decisions freely about what they wish to import or export.

questions.text: When did the Indian government start the policy of liberalisation in its economic policies?
questions.explanation: In 1991, India removed many barriers to foreign trade and foreign investment.

questions.text: What does WTO stand for?
questions.explanation: WTO is an international organization that deals with the global rules of trade between nations.

questions.text: The main aim of the WTO is to:
questions.explanation: WTO works towards ensuring that trade flows as smoothly, predictably and freely as possible.

questions.text: One major criticism of the WTO is that it:
options: Forces developing countries to remove barriers while developed countries keep them
questions.explanation: Developing countries argue that trade rules are often biased in favor of developed nations.

questions.text: SEZs (Special Economic Zones) are being set up by the Central and State governments in India to:
options: Attract foreign companies to invest in India
questions.explanation: SEZs provide world-class facilities and tax exemptions to attract investors.

questions.text: One way in which MNCs exert control over production is by placing orders for production with ______.
questions.explanation: MNCs in industries like garments and footwear often outsource production to small local units.

questions.text: Which of the following is a negative impact of globalisation for small producers?
options: Competition leading to closure of units
questions.explanation: Small Indian industries like batteries, capacitors, and toys have suffered due to competition from cheap imports.

questions.text: Globalisation has led to an improvement in the living standards of:
questions.explanation: The benefits of globalisation have been unevenly distributed, primarily favoring the wealthy and urban populations.

questions.text: Flexibility in ______ laws has been introduced to attract foreign investment.
questions.explanation: Governments have allowed companies to hire workers for short periods to reduce labor costs.

questions.text: A fair globalisation would create ______ for all.
questions.explanation: Fair globalisation ensures that the benefits are shared better and there is protection for workers' rights.

questions.text: Who can play a major role in the struggle for a fair globalisation?
questions.explanation: Governments can use policies to protect the interests of their people and negotiate for fairer rules at the WTO.

questions.text: Foreign investment is different from domestic investment because it involves ______.
questions.explanation: It represents the flow of capital across national boundaries.

questions.text: Ford Motors is an MNC because:
options: It is a very old company
It has manufacturing plants in many countries like India
questions.explanation: Control of production in multiple nations is the defining characteristic.

questions.text: By 2017, how many countries were members of the WTO?
questions.explanation: The WTO has a vast membership comprising most of the world's trading nations.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an example of a barrier on foreign trade?
questions.explanation: The first three are tools used by governments to restrict or regulate trade.

questions.text: Indian buyers now have a wide choice of goods because of:
questions.explanation: Integration of global markets has brought a variety of international brands to India.

questions.text: MNCs are interested in investing in India because of its:
questions.explanation: India offers a huge consumer base and a competitive cost of production.

questions.text: In the past two decades, globalisation has seen much more movement of ______ between countries.
questions.explanation: While people move, the volume of trade and capital flows has been the most significant aspect of globalisation.

questions.text: The entry of MNCs in the service sector in India has created new jobs in fields like:
questions.explanation: Services that can be off-shored have seen significant growth in India.

questions.text: MNCs sometimes set up production jointly with some of the local companies. The main benefit to the local company is:
options: MNCs provide money for additional investments and latest technology
questions.explanation: Partnerships allow local companies to upgrade their production standards.

questions.text: Globalisation has also created new ______ for companies providing services.
questions.explanation: Indian IT and software companies have gained immensely from global demand.

questions.text: Which of the following is a 'Top Indian Company' that has emerged as an MNC?
questions.explanation: Many Indian companies have expanded their operations to various countries across the globe.

questions.text: Small producers who were unable to compete with MNCs had to ______ their units.
questions.explanation: Competition from large-scale, low-cost global production is often fatal for small-scale local units.

questions.text: Under the influence of MNCs, jobs for many workers have become ______.
questions.explanation: The pressure of competition and cost-cutting has led to a rise in informal and temporary employment.

questions.text: The government of India can support small producers to improve their performance until they become ______ enough to compete.
questions.explanation: Protecting 'infant' industries is a common strategy in developing nations.

questions.text: World Trade Organisation (WTO) is criticized for ______ the developing countries to open their economies.
questions.explanation: Many believe the WTO acts as a tool for rich nations to access markets in poorer countries.

questions.text: Foreign trade and integration of markets results in ______ of prices of similar goods in two markets.
questions.explanation: Competition tends to bring prices closer across different regional markets.
